title: Ancient Humans in Australia: A DNA Discovery
summary: Scientists have found that humans first arrived in Australia and New Guinea about 60,000 years ago. Long ago, during the Ice Age, these places were connected as one big land called Sahul. People have wondered for a long time when humans got there. Researchers from universities in the UK worked together to study DNA from people today, like Aboriginal Australians and New Guineans. They looked at a special type of DNA called mitochondrial DNA, which comes from mothers. By comparing DNA from different groups, they built a family tree that goes way back in time. The DNA showed that the oldest family lines started around 60,000 years ago. This means humans came earlier than some thought. The study also found that these early humans probably came from Southeast Asia, using different routes to reach Sahul. This helps us understand how people spread across the world long ago.

keywords.explanation: a special code in our bodies that tells us about our family history
the old name for when Australia and New Guinea were joined together as one land
a type of DNA passed down from mothers, used to trace family lines
a time long ago when the world was much colder and sea levels were lower
the paths people took to move from one place to another

questions.question: How long ago did humans first arrive in Australia according to the DNA study?
What was the name of the ancient land that included Australia and New Guinea?
What type of DNA did scientists study to learn about early humans?
Where did the early humans likely come from before reaching Sahul?
How did scientists use DNA to figure out when humans arrived?
options: By building a family tree and using a molecular clock
questions.correct_answer: By building a family tree and using a molecular clock

questions.question: What does the study suggest about how early humans reached Sahul?
options: They used at least two separate routes
They all came on one big boat
questions.correct_answer: They used at least two separate routes

background_read: Long ago, during the Ice Age, the world was different. Sea levels were lower, and lands like Australia and New Guinea were connected as Sahul. People have always been curious about when humans first settled there. Scientists study DNA, which is like a family history book inside us, to learn about the past. By looking at DNA from Aboriginal Australians and New Guineans today, they can trace back to ancient times. This helps us understand how humans moved and lived thousands of years ago, showing that they were great explorers even without modern tools.
Article_Structure: WHO: Researchers from universities in the UK, like Professor Martin Richards and Professor Helen Farr, studied DNA from Aboriginal Australians and New Guineans.
WHAT: They found that humans first arrived in Australia and New Guinea about 60,000 years ago, using DNA evidence to support this.
WHERE: The study focused on Sahul (ancient Australia and New Guinea) and Southeast Asia where early humans came from.
WHY: To solve the debate about when humans settled Sahul and understand their migration routes from Southeast Asia.

perspectives.description: Scientists use DNA and research to learn about ancient human history and migration.
